mnemonics.title: Process States
mnemonics.content: NEW → READY → RUNNING → WAITING → TERMINATED. REMEMBER: NEVER WAIT - GET READY TO RUN A NEW TERMINAL (acronym helps recall flow: New-Ready-Running-Waiting-Terminated). A simpler mnemonic: N-ow R-eady? W-ait T-ill R-un N-ot?

mnemonics.title: PCB Components
mnemonics.content: PID STATE PC REG MEM I/O ACCT - Remember as: Process Saves Everything Required For Memory And Input/Output Operations. Or: P-SPARC-MIA (Process State, Program Counter, Registers, Memory management, I/O status, Accounting).

mnemonics.title: Memory Layout
mnemonics.content: TEXT DATA HEAP STACK - Remember as: The High School Text: Top (Text), Dogs (Data), Have (Heap), Stacks (Stack). Another: He Tells Dads He Studies (Heap, Text, Data, Heap, Stack).

mnemonics.title: Process vs Program
mnemonics.content: Program = PASSIVE (P lies on disk). Process = ACTIVE (A ctively running). P for Program = Passive. A for Active = Process.

acronyms.usage: Kernel data structure storing all process information - like a process passport containing identity, state, and resources.
Unique number assigned to each process by OS - like a student's roll number in a class.
PID of the process that created this process - like father's Aadhar card number linked to child's.
Identifies the user who owns the process - determines file access permissions and resource limits.
Register pointing to next instruction to execute - like a bookmark showing current page in a book.
The processor that executes process instructions - the brain of the computer system.
Data transfer between process and external devices - like reading/writing through doors and windows.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a queue at a movie theater ticket counter. NEW = people just arriving (filling form). READY = people in line waiting. RUNNING = person at counter buying ticket. WAITING = person called aside for ID verification. TERMINATED = person left with ticket.

Think of PCB as a process DOSSER (like a dossier or file folder) containing: ID card (PID), Current activity report (state), Bookmark (PC), Notes (registers), Address book (memory pointers), Status report (I/O info), History (accounting).

Imagine a multi-story building: Ground floor = Stack (function calls, temporary), 1st floor = Heap (dynamic furniture), 2nd floor = Data (stored items), Top floor = Text (blueprints/instructions). Building can expand (heap grows up) or shrink (stack grows down).

Think of CPU as a desk and processes as students taking turns. When switching: Student A puts their work in their locker (save state to PCB), Student B takes their work from their locker (load state from PCB), Both use the same desk but different materials.
Imagine a photocopier (fork). Original document = parent process. Photocopy = child process. Both have identical content initially but can be edited independently after copying.

analogies.analogy: Program is like a recipe written in a cookbook (passive). Process is like actually cooking the recipe in a kitchen - using ingredients, utensils, and following steps (active). Same recipe can be cooked multiple times creating different processes.
Like a student's school record containing: admission number (PID), current class (state), last page read in textbook (program counter), attendance record (accounting), library card status (I/O info).

A student graduates (process terminates) but their seat in the classroom remains marked with their name until the teacher (parent) checks their marks (exit status). Until then, the 'graduated student' is like a zombie - exists in records but not actually present.

A child whose parents die becomes an orphan and gets adopted by a guardian (init process). The child continues to exist and work (running) but now reports to the new guardian instead of original parents.

A juggler (CPU) keeps multiple balls (processes) in the air. When switching from ball A to ball B, the juggler briefly pauses ball A's pattern (saves state) and starts ball B's pattern (restores state) - this pause is the context switch.

A cell dividing (mitosis). Parent cell copies its entire genetic material (address space) to create an identical daughter cell. After division, both cells can develop differently (execute different code via exec()).
